description.wishlist: A job you've saved and might apply to — nothing sent yet.
description.draft: You're preparing the application but haven't submitted it.
description.applied: Application submitted — waiting to hear back.
description.phone_screen: First call with a recruiter or hiring manager.
description.technical: Technical interview or take-home assignment stage.
description.final_round: Last interview stage before the company decides.
description.offer: The company made an offer — the decision is yours now.
description.accepted: You took their offer — the happy ending.
description.rejected: The company decided not to move forward.
description.ghosted: The company went silent and stopped responding.
description.withdrawn: You pulled out of the process yourself.
description.archived: Closed and hidden from day-to-day tracking.

field.status_inclusion: New applications can only start as wishlist, draft, or applied.

field.resume_too_long: The resume must be under 1 MB.

field.cover_letter_too_long: The cover letter must be under 1 MB.
field.cover_letter_not_a_pdf: The cover letter must be a PDF.

home.eyebrow: A portfolio project by Chairul Akmal
home.headline: A job tracker built on a <accent>finite state machine</accent>.
home.lede: Thirteen states, an explicit transition table, and an immutable audit trail behind every change. Track an application from wishlist to offer without the pipeline ever lying to you about where it is.
home.pipelineNote: It is not a line. A job hunt goes quiet, or ends, and then starts again — so three closed states can return to Applied, and the audit trail keeps every step of the round trip.
home.pipelineSource: Illustration. The transition table lives in api/app/lib/application_fsm.rb
home.fsmBody: Thirteen states and a hand-written list of the moves between them. ghosted may return to applied; applied may not jump to offer. It is a plain Ruby object, not a state-machine gem.
home.auditBody: Every transition appends a timeline entry — who moved it, from which state, to which, and when. Nothing in the app updates one in place.
home.jobsBody: Follow-up reminders run on Solid Queue inside the Puma process. No Redis, no worker service. Each reminder is idempotent by key, so a retry cannot send it twice.
home.tagline: A job application tracker built on a finite state machine: thirteen states, an explicit transition table, and an immutable audit trail. Rails 8 API, Next.js 16 frontend, one PostgreSQL database.

about.title: Why it is built this way
about.lede: KarirKalyan tracks job applications. It is also where I settled four questions before writing any of it. Each one had an easier answer than the one I took, so here is what the easier answer would have cost.
about.railsTitle: Why Rails, when I write TypeScript
about.railsBody: My working life has been JavaScript. Tokyo runs on Rails, and you learn less about a framework's conventions by reading them than by shipping something inside them. So the API is Rails 8 — API-only, Devise and devise-jwt for auth — while the frontend stayed the Next.js I already knew. The unfamiliar half of the stack is the half I set out to learn, and it is the half with the tests.
about.fsmTitle: Why a plain object, not a state-machine gem
about.fsmBody: ApplicationFSM is a plain Ruby object wrapping a table of legal transitions. A gem would have given the same guarantees and put a DSL between me and them. Thirteen states fit in one readable array — including the revival edges, where ghosted, rejected and withdrawn each return to applied, because real job hunts do that. Transitions run through a service object rather than a model callback, so every status change in the system has a call site you can grep for.
about.jobsTitle: Why Solid Queue, not Sidekiq and Redis
about.jobsBody: One job needs to run once a day. Sidekiq would have meant a Redis instance and a worker process — two more things to deploy, monitor and pay for, in service of a single cron. Solid Queue keeps the queue in the Postgres database that already exists, and its workers run inside the Puma process. The deployed system is two services and one database, which is the whole argument.
about.filesBody: Resumes and cover letters are stored as bytea, capped at one megabyte, and served through dedicated download endpoints that keep them out of the ordinary JSON. Object storage is the right answer at scale and the wrong one here: a bucket, a set of credentials, and a signed-URL dance, for files that are small and few. Storing them in the row means the file and the record are backed up together — worth more, at this size, than a ceiling I will not reach.
about.closingBody: The demo account is seeded with twelve Tokyo tech applications spread across the pipeline. The source is public, and the API documents itself.

docs.title: The API, and how it behaves
docs.lede: The frontend you are reading is one client of a Rails 8 JSON API. Every endpoint is documented by its own request spec — the OpenAPI schema is generated from the tests, so it cannot drift from the implementation. This page is the shape of the thing; the Swagger UI is the reference.
docs.authBody: Sign-up and sign-in return a JWT in the Authorization header. In this frontend that token never reaches client-side JavaScript: a Next.js route handler receives it and writes an httpOnly cookie, and every later request is proxied server-side. Signing out rotates the token's jti, which revokes every device at once.
docs.scopeBody: Every record is reached through current_user.applications. Asking for someone else's application returns 404, not 403 — a 403 would confirm the row exists.
docs.errorsBody: Every failure responds with a single error key holding one string, never an array. Validation failures join their messages into it. The status code carries the meaning:

errorTable.409: An optimistic-locking conflict — the row changed under you.
errorTable.422: An illegal FSM transition, a validation failure, or a bad pre-fill URL.
errorTable.503: The AI key is missing, or Postgres is down.

docs.paginationBody: The applications list takes ?after=…&limit=20 — limit clamped to 1–100, default 10 — and answers with a data array beside a meta object carrying next_cursor and has_more. The cursor is a Base64 timestamp. A malformed one is ignored and you get the first page rather than an error. Filters compose with it server-side.

endpoint.signOut: Revoke the token on every device.
endpoint.index: List your applications, paginated and filterable.
endpoint.create: Create one. The status must be an entry state.
endpoint.prefill: Read a job posting URL and fill the fields with Claude.
endpoint.show: One application, with its valid next states and timeline.
endpoint.update: Edit the fields. Not the status — that is a transition.
endpoint.transition: Move the status through the state machine.
endpoint.resume: Download the stored resume as a PDF.
endpoint.coverLetter: Download the stored cover letter as a PDF.
endpoint.dashboard: Aggregate counts and the filter facets.

docs.swaggerBody: Request and response schemas, every parameter, and a console to call the API live. Generated by rswag from the request specs.

auth.expired: Your session expired. Sign in again to continue.

dashboard.avgDaysTooltip: Counts applications that reached offer, accepted, or declined. Measured from your applied date to when the offer status was recorded in the audit log.

detail.finalState: {label} — {description} This is a final state, so the status can no longer change.

transitions.reopenable: Can be re-opened to Applied later.

files.tooLarge: That file is {size} MB — the limit is 1 MB.

newApplication.statusHint: You can advance the status later from the application page.
